Sophia_temp-analysis

Allison Davis and Sophia McKelvey

2024-02-22

Temperature experiment

Purpose: Our interest lies in whether sexually selected traits have deleterious effects on males’ ability to tolerate change. This is from an observation of males dying when sampling during hot months but females surviving fine.

Hypothesis: (1) Sexually selected traits decrease resistance to environmental change across species; and (2) courtship displays further decrease resistance to environmental change within a species.

Predictions: - Males of fish with elaborate traits have a narrower thermal tolerance–i.e. lower thermal maximums and higher thermal minimums–compared to females of these species. - Males of fish without elaborate traits will have a similar tolerance to females of the same species. - Males performing courtship displays have a narrower thermal tolerance compared to males using coercion (within swordtails).

Data

We performed crtical temperature assays in which we exposed fish in groups of about 8 to gradually increasing or decreasing temperatures. Individual critical temperatures were recorded at the time of loss of equilibrium (LOE). We will then compare these temperatures, and the total thermal tolerance (CTmax-CTmin), both within species and between species.

To work with the data, we first need to create a github repository, upload a csv of our data, and then pull the data down into this Rmd to analyse it.

You first need to call you data from the internet (github) in order to run stats in Rstudio. Your data should be saved as a csv file, and uploaded to your github repository (github project). On github, click on your project, click on the csv file, then hit “raw” –you will need to copy the html of the raw file to use in Rstudio.

To pull your data into Rstudio, you will need to use the following functions/packages: library(), curl package, curl(), read.csv()

  • If you haven’t done so already, install the curl package. You can install a package in two ways:

    1. use install.packages() with the name of the package in the brackets

    2. go to your packages tab in the lower right corner (Files Plots Packages Help Viewer), hit install, and type in the name of the package before hitting the install button again.

#load in the curl package
library(curl)
## Using libcurl 8.3.0 with Schannel
#pull the data from the internet using your html and assign a name
rawdata_1 <- curl("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sophia-m0823/TestRStudio/main/Temp%20ID%20-%20Completed%20Data.csv")

#force your data to be read as a csv
rawdata_1 <- read.csv(rawdata_1, header = TRUE, sep = ",", stringsAsFactors = TRUE)
head(rawdata_1)
##        Species   Mating Size.cat    Sex     Name CTmax.num CTmin.num CTmax.C
## 1 X. nigrensis courting    large   male   Charms         5         2   36.06
## 2 X. nigrensis courting    large   male Cinnamon         6         3   36.25
## 3 X. nigrensis courting    large   male   Crunch         3         5   35.94
## 4 X. nigrensis     <NA>      reg female    Lucky         4         1   35.88
## 5 X. nigrensis     <NA>      reg female    Toast         1         4   32.13
## 6 X. nigrensis     <NA>      reg female Cherrios         2         6   33.25
##   CTmin.C T.tot Size.mm
## 1    9.81 26.25    34.0
## 2    9.38 26.87    32.8
## 3    9.19 26.75    33.0
## 4   10.56 25.32    21.3
## 5    9.31 22.82    23.9
## 6    8.56 24.69    24.8

Now we want to make sure your data was pulled correctly, and we also want to create separate data frames for each of the species. This way we can do stats on four data frames: raw data (original file we pulled from the internet), sailfin (all the info but just for P. latipinna), mosquitofish (all the info but just for G. affinis), and swordtails (all the info but just for the X. nigrensis).

For this step, you will need: head(), data frame selection (aka data frame[data frame$name …..])

  • Make sure you assign a new name for your new data frames! If you keep the name the same for all three data frames, R will overwrite (aka erase) your data!
  • For more info on how to select from a data frame, see: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/lessR/vignettes/Extract.html#:~:text=The%20most%20general%20way%20to,d%5Brows%2C%20columms%5D%20.
#check the head of your raw dataframe to make sure it was read in correctly
#NOTE: whenever you read in a csv, R automatically adds X.U.FEFF to the first column. This is normal!

sailfin_1 <- rawdata_1[rawdata_1$Species =="P. latipinna",]   ### first = row, then column


#create a dataframe for mosquitofish by selecting data from your raw dataframe with G. affinis as the species.
mosquito_1 <- rawdata_1[rawdata_1$Species =="G. affinis",]

#create a dataframe for swordtails by selecting data from your raw dataframe with X. nigrensis as the species.
swordtail_1 <- rawdata_1[rawdata_1$Species =="X. nigrensis",]

Analysis

Summary stats

Now we get to the fun part -actually analyzing our data! While most people want to jump right in to the most complicated stat models, it’s actually better practice to get an idea of what your data look like. Getting summary statistics (mean, median, mode etc) and visualizing your data give you an idea of how your data relate to each other. This will make the conclusions from the more complicated stats easier to understand.

So let’s begin with summary statistics. For this you will need: rstatix package, get_summary_stats()

  • Make sure to install the rstatix package. See the beginning of this Rmd on how to install.

  • The summary stats you want are: mean, standard deviation, median, and interquartile range.

  • For more details on how to use get_summary_stats() see: https://www.datanovia.com/en/lessons/wilcoxon-test-in-r/#summary-statistics-2

    • ex: get_summary_stats(weight, type =“median”, data=mice)
      • the %>% used on the website requires another package (tidyverse), but is just specifying the data frame to use; if you’re not familiar with tidyverse, instead use data=name in function OR specify by mice$weight.

  • You may need to switch from a ‘wide’ format dataframe to a ‘long’ format dataframe in order from some of these functions to work. For this you will use the ‘pivot_longer()’ and ‘pivot_wider()’ functions from tidyverse (don’t worry, if you’re not familar with tidyverse, you can just follow the info in the link). For more info on how to use, see: https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/articles/pivot.html

#load in the rstatix package 

library(rstatix)
## 
## Attaching package: 'rstatix'
## The following object is masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter
#get summary statistics for all data
summary(rawdata_1)
##          Species         Mating    Size.cat      Sex          Name   
##  G. affinis  :17   coercive :17   large: 7   female:39   Bella  : 3  
##  P. latipinna:32   courting :23   reg  :64   male  :40   Jess   : 3  
##  X. nigrensis:30   NA's     :39   small: 8               Amy    : 2  
##                                                          Beth   : 2  
##                                                          Brad   : 2  
##                                                          Chad   : 2  
##                                                          (Other):65  
##    CTmax.num       CTmin.num        CTmax.C         CTmin.C      
##  Min.   :1.000   Min.   :1.000   Min.   :32.13   Min.   : 2.060  
##  1st Qu.:2.500   1st Qu.:2.500   1st Qu.:35.78   1st Qu.: 6.565  
##  Median :4.000   Median :4.000   Median :37.63   Median : 7.880  
##  Mean   :4.481   Mean   :4.481   Mean   :37.05   Mean   : 7.597  
##  3rd Qu.:6.000   3rd Qu.:6.000   3rd Qu.:38.44   3rd Qu.: 9.380  
##  Max.   :9.000   Max.   :9.000   Max.   :39.75   Max.   :11.130  
##                                                                  
##      T.tot          Size.mm     
##  Min.   :22.12   Min.   :17.45  
##  1st Qu.:26.03   1st Qu.:22.30  
##  Median :29.62   Median :29.70  
##  Mean   :29.45   Mean   :28.36  
##  3rd Qu.:32.34   3rd Qu.:33.35  
##  Max.   :36.44   Max.   :38.70  
## 
#get summary statistics for the sailfin data  #NOTE: want to get an idea of how each species looks, so we'll separate them.
summary(sailfin_1)
##          Species         Mating    Size.cat      Sex          Name   
##  G. affinis  : 0   coercive : 0   large: 0   female:16   Jess   : 2  
##  P. latipinna:32   courting :16   reg  :32   male  :16   Amy    : 1  
##  X. nigrensis: 0   NA's     :16   small: 0               August : 1  
##                                                          Bella  : 1  
##                                                          Bert   : 1  
##                                                          Beth   : 1  
##                                                          (Other):25  
##    CTmax.num      CTmin.num       CTmax.C         CTmin.C          T.tot      
##  Min.   :1.00   Min.   :1.00   Min.   :36.81   Min.   :3.560   Min.   :28.43  
##  1st Qu.:2.75   1st Qu.:2.75   1st Qu.:37.97   1st Qu.:6.160   1st Qu.:30.13  
##  Median :4.50   Median :4.50   Median :38.44   Median :7.250   Median :31.25  
##  Mean   :4.50   Mean   :4.50   Mean   :38.44   Mean   :6.884   Mean   :31.56  
##  3rd Qu.:6.25   3rd Qu.:6.25   3rd Qu.:39.06   3rd Qu.:7.827   3rd Qu.:32.84  
##  Max.   :8.00   Max.   :8.00   Max.   :39.75   Max.   :9.250   Max.   :35.50  
##                                                                               
##     Size.mm     
##  Min.   :26.70  
##  1st Qu.:31.50  
##  Median :33.70  
##  Mean   :33.12  
##  3rd Qu.:35.20  
##  Max.   :38.70  
## 
#get summary statistics for the mosquitofish data
summary(mosquito_1)
##          Species         Mating   Size.cat      Sex         Name   
##  G. affinis  :17   coercive :9   large: 0   female:8   Ahoy   : 1  
##  P. latipinna: 0   courting :0   reg  :17   male  :9   Cheez  : 1

summary(swordtail_1)

Summary plots

While numbers are great, a picture is often easier to understand. So let’s graph out our data to get a better idea of what’s going on. Box plots are a good graph to use, since it will show us the median and range of the data.

We’ll focus on each species separately, then look at them all together. Our main goal is to see if males and females differ in their thermal tolerance.

You may need to switch from a ‘wide’ format dataframe to a ‘long’ format dataframe in order from some of these functions to work. For this you will use the ‘pivot_longer()’ and ‘pivot_wider()’ functions from tidyverse (don’t worry, if you’re not familar with tidyverse, you can just follow the info in the link). For more info on how to use, see: https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/articles/pivot.html

For this you will use: boxplot(), line() [can also use ggplot if you’re more comfortable with that]

  • For more info on boxplot() see: https://www.statmethods.net/graphs/boxplot.html

  • For more info on line() see: https://r-graph-gallery.com/line-plot.html

  • We want our boxplots to temperature by sex for within species, and temperature by sex AND species for between species. When we want to display our y value (temp) by two x values (sex and species), we denote this as y~x1*x2. We’ll repeat this for our Tmax, Tmin, and Ttot temperature vaues.

  • Remember to add x and y lables!

library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
## ✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.3     ✔ tibble    3.2.1
## ✔ purrr     1.0.2     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter()     masks rstatix::filter(), stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()        masks stats::lag()
## ✖ readr::parse_date() masks curl::parse_date()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
#create a box plot showing max temperature by sex in sailfin
head(sailfin_1)
##         Species   Mating Size.cat    Sex    Name CTmax.num CTmin.num CTmax.C
## 16 P. latipinna courting      reg   male   Onion         2         2   38.50
## 17 P. latipinna courting      reg   male Cheddar         5         3   39.06
## 18 P. latipinna courting      reg   male     Bun         7         7   39.44
## 19 P. latipinna courting      reg   male  Pickle         8         1   39.75
## 20 P. latipinna     <NA>      reg female  Tomato         1         4   38.19
## 21 P. latipinna     <NA>      reg female   Patti         3         6   38.69
##    CTmin.C   T.tot Size.mm
## 16  4.6875 33.8125    33.3
## 17  4.6900 34.3700    34.2
## 18  4.6300 34.8100    32.9
## 19  4.6900 35.0600    34.3
## 20  4.5600 33.6300    32.4
## 21  4.6900 34.0000    32.8
sailfin_boxplot1 <- ggplot(data = sailfin_1, 
                           aes(x = Sex,
                               y = CTmax.C,
                               fill = Sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Temperatures by Sex in Sailfins",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Temperature (°C)"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue"))
  

print(sailfin_boxplot1)

# repeat for min temperature, and Ttot
sailfin_boxplot2 <- ggplot(data = sailfin_1, 
                           aes(x = Sex,
                               y = CTmin.C,
                               fill = Sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Minimum Temperatures by Sex in Sailfins",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Temperature (°C)"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue"))

print(sailfin_boxplot2)

sailfin_boxplot3 <- ggplot(data = sailfin_1, 
                           aes(x = Sex,
                               y = T.tot,
                               fill = Sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Total Thermal Range by Sex in Sailfins",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Thermal Range"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue"))

print(sailfin_boxplot3)

#create a box plot showing max temperature by sex in mosquitofish
mosquito_boxplot1 <- ggplot(data = mosquito_1, 
                           aes(x = Sex,
                               y = CTmax.C,
                               fill = Sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Temperatures by Sex in Mosquito Fish",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Temperature (°C)"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue"))

print(mosquito_boxplot1)

# repeat for min temperature, and Ttot
mosquito_boxplot2 <- ggplot(data = mosquito_1, 
                           aes(x = Sex,
                               y = CTmin.C,
                               fill = Sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Minimum Temperatures by Sex in Mosquito Fish",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Temperature (°C)"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue"))

print(mosquito_boxplot2)

mosquito_boxplot3 <- ggplot(data = mosquito_1, 
                           aes(x = Sex,
                               y = T.tot,
                               fill = Sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Total Thermal Range by Sex in Mosquito Fish",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Thermal Range"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue"))

print(mosquito_boxplot3)

#create a box plot showing max temperature by sex in swordtail

swordtail_boxplot1 <- ggplot(data = swordtail_1, 
                           aes(x = Sex,
                               y = CTmax.C,
                               fill = Sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Temperatures by Sex in Swordtails",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Temperature (°C)"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue"))

print(swordtail_boxplot1)

# repeat for min temperature, and Ttot

swordtail_boxplot2 <- ggplot(data = swordtail_1, 
                           aes(x = Sex,
                               y = CTmin.C,
                               fill = Sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Minimum Temperatures by Sex in Swordtails",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Temperature (°C)"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue"))

print(swordtail_boxplot2)

swordtail_boxplot3 <- ggplot(data = swordtail_1, 
                           aes(x = Sex,
                               y = T.tot,
                               fill = Sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Total Thermal Range by Sex in Swordtails",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Thermal Range"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue"))

print(swordtail_boxplot3)

#create a box plot showing max temperature by sex AND size category in swordtails
swordtail_boxplot4 <- ggplot(data = swordtail_1, 
                             aes(x = interaction(Sex, Size.cat),
                                 y = CTmax.C,
                                 fill = interaction(Sex, Size.cat))) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Temperatures by Sex and Size in Swordtails",
    x = "Sex and Size Category",
    y = "Temperature (°C)",
    fill = "Legend"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue", "forestgreen"))

print(swordtail_boxplot4)

# repeat for min temperature, and Ttot
swordtail_boxplot5 <- ggplot(data = swordtail_1, 
                             aes(x = interaction(Sex, Size.cat),
                                 y = CTmin.C,
                                 fill = interaction(Sex, Size.cat))) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Minimum Temperatures by Sex and Size in Swordtails",
    x = "Sex and Size Category",
    y = "Temperature (°C)",
    fill = "Legend"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue", "forestgreen"))

print(swordtail_boxplot5)

swordtail_boxplot6 <- ggplot(data = swordtail_1, 
                             aes(x = interaction(Sex, Size.cat),
                                 y = T.tot,
                                 fill = interaction(Sex, Size.cat))) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Total Thermal Range by Sex and Size in Swordtails",
    x = "Sex and Size Category",
    y = "Thermal Range",
    fill = "Legend"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue", "forestgreen"))

print(swordtail_boxplot6)

#create a box plot showing max temperature by sex AND species (all)   
all_boxplot1 <- ggplot(data = rawdata_1, 
                             aes(x = interaction(Sex, Species),
                                 y = CTmax.C,
                                 fill = interaction(Sex, Species))) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Temperatures by Sex and Species",
    x = "Sex and Species",
    y = "Temperature (°C)",
    fill = "Legend"
  ) +
  theme_classic()

print(all_boxplot1)

# repeat for min temperature, and Ttot
all_boxplot2 <- ggplot(data = rawdata_1, 
                             aes(x = interaction(Sex, Species),
                                 y = CTmin.C,
                                 fill = interaction(Sex, Species))) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Minimum Temperatures by Sex and Species",
    x = "Sex and Species",
    y = "Temperature (°C)",
    fill = "Legend"
  ) +
  theme_classic()

print(all_boxplot2)

all_boxplot3 <- ggplot(data = rawdata_1, 
                             aes(x = interaction(Sex, Species),
                                 y = T.tot,
                                 fill = interaction(Sex, Species))) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Total Thermal Range by Sex and Species",
    x = "Sex and Species",
    y = "Thermal Range",
    fill = "Legend"
  ) +
  theme_classic()

print(all_boxplot3)

#create a line plot showing temperature by sex AND species
library(ggplot2)
all_lineplot1 <- ggplot(data = rawdata_1, 
                        aes(x = factor(Sex),
                            y = CTmax.C,
                            group = Species,
                            color = Species)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Temperature by Sex and Species",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_classic()

print(all_lineplot1)

# repeat for min temperature, and Ttot
all_lineplot2 <- ggplot(data = rawdata_1, 
                        aes(x = factor(Sex),
                            y = CTmin.C,
                            group = Species,
                            color = Species)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Minimum Temperature by Sex and Species",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_classic()

print(all_lineplot2)

all_lineplot3 <- ggplot(data = rawdata_1, 
                        aes(x = factor(Sex),
                            y = T.tot,
                            group = Species,
                            color = Species)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Total Thermal Range by Sex and Species",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Thermal Range") +
  theme_classic()

print(all_lineplot3)

Now that you’ve done basic statistics, how does your data look? Write about the trends you see below.

FIRST LOOK AT DATA:

Comparisons

In our analysis, we want to answer the following questions:

  1. How do males and females differ in their max, min and overall thermal tolerance?

  2. How do the species differ? Do males and females only differ in particular species?

  3. How do courting species differ from non-courting species? Is there a difference regardless of sex? Is there a difference between the two types of males even within the same species (eg swordtails)?

Normality check

Before we begin, we need to check if our data follows a normal distribution. A lot of the parametric tests (t-tests, ANOVAs, etc) have normality as a requirement, so we need confirm our data fits this. If not, we might need to transform the data or perform non-parametric tests.

You will need to check for normality across the entire dataset. We will do this three ways: using a Shariro-Wilke test, histograms, and QQ plots.

For this you will use: dplyr package, shapiro.test(), hist(), qqnorm(), and qqline()

  • For more info on the Shapiro-Wilke tests, see: https://www.statology.org/shapiro-wilk-test-r/

  • For more info on histograms, see: https://www.datamentor.io/r-programming/histogram

  • For more info on QQ plots, see: http://www.sthda.com/english/wiki/qq-plots-quantile-quantile-plots-r-base-graphs

#perform SW tests for entire dataset
library(dplyr)
shapiro.test(rawdata_1$CTmax.C)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  rawdata_1$CTmax.C
## W = 0.94199, p-value = 0.001337
shapiro.test(rawdata_1$CTmin.C)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  rawdata_1$CTmin.C
## W = 0.93528, p-value = 0.0005928
shapiro.test(rawdata_1$T.tot)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  rawdata_1$T.tot
## W = 0.95666, p-value = 0.009034
shapiro.test(rawdata_1$Size.mm)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  rawdata_1$Size.mm
## W = 0.92219, p-value = 0.0001334
#Create histogram for entire dataset
hist(rawdata_1$CTmax.C, col = 'blue')

hist(rawdata_1$CTmin.C, col = 'red')

hist(rawdata_1$T.tot, col = 'purple')

hist(rawdata_1$Size.mm, col = 'yellow')

#Create QQplot for entire dataset
qqnorm(rawdata_1$CTmax.C, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(rawdata_1$CTmax.C, col = "blue", lwd = 2)

qqnorm(rawdata_1$CTmin.C, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(rawdata_1$CTmin.C, col = "red", lwd = 2)

qqnorm(rawdata_1$T.tot, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(rawdata_1$T.tot, col = "purple", lwd = 2)

qqnorm(rawdata_1$Size.mm, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(rawdata_1$Size.mm, col = "yellow", lwd = 2)

How does your data look? Normal, sorta normal, super not normal? Write about the trends you see below.

NORMALITY CHECK:

Analysis

To address our questions, we will first examine our data in small chunks (within species), then take a look at the whole picture (between species) using mixed effects models.

NOTE: if the data was not normal, we need to transform it before continuing. Typical transformations include log or square root transformations. Transform data, and retest for normality. You can ignore this if the data was normal.

## log transformation
log_CTmax <- log(rawdata_1$CTmax.C, base = 10)
log_CTmin <- log(rawdata_1$CTmin.C, base = 10)
log_T.tot <- log(rawdata_1$T.tot, base = 10)
log_size.mm <- log(rawdata_1$Size.mm, base = 10)

## sqrt transformation
sqrt_CTmax <- sqrt(rawdata_1$CTmax.C)
sqrt_CTmin <- sqrt(rawdata_1$CTmin.C)
sqrt_T.tot <- sqrt(rawdata_1$T.tot)
sqrt_size.mm <- sqrt(rawdata_1$Size.mm)

## cube root transformation
cube_CTmax <- '^'(rawdata_1$CTmax.C, 1/3)
cube_CTmin <- '^'(rawdata_1$CTmin.C, 1/3)
cube_T.tot <-'^'(rawdata_1$T.tot, 1/3)
cube_size.mm <- '^'(rawdata_1$Size.mm, 1/3)


#redo SW test
shapiro.test(log_CTmax)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  log_CTmax
## W = 0.93643, p-value = 0.0006794
shapiro.test(log_CTmin)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  log_CTmin
## W = 0.85674, p-value = 3.144e-07
shapiro.test(log_T.tot)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  log_T.tot
## W = 0.95845, p-value = 0.01154
shapiro.test(log_size.mm)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  log_size.mm
## W = 0.91359, p-value = 5.325e-05
shapiro.test(sqrt_CTmax)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  sqrt_CTmax
## W = 0.93935, p-value = 0.0009664
shapiro.test(sqrt_CTmin)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  sqrt_CTmin
## W = 0.90385, p-value = 1.985e-05
shapiro.test(sqrt_T.tot)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  sqrt_T.tot
## W = 0.95817, p-value = 0.01111
shapiro.test(sqrt_size.mm)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  sqrt_size.mm
## W = 0.91879, p-value = 9.219e-05
shapiro.test(cube_CTmax)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  cube_CTmax
## W = 0.9384, p-value = 0.000862
shapiro.test(cube_CTmin)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  cube_CTmin
## W = 0.88994, p-value = 5.288e-06
shapiro.test(cube_T.tot)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  cube_T.tot
## W = 0.9584, p-value = 0.01147
shapiro.test(cube_size.mm)
## 
##  Shapiro-Wilk normality test
## 
## data:  cube_size.mm
## W = 0.91726, p-value = 7.832e-05
#redo histogram
hist(log_CTmax, col = 'blue')

hist(log_CTmin, col = 'red')

hist(log_T.tot, col = 'purple')

hist(log_size.mm, col = 'yellow')

hist(sqrt_CTmax, col = 'blue')

hist(sqrt_CTmin, col = 'red')

hist(sqrt_T.tot, col = 'purple')

hist(sqrt_size.mm, col = 'yellow')

#redo QQplot
qqnorm(log_CTmax, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(log_CTmax, col = "blue", lwd = 2)

qqnorm(log_CTmin, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(log_CTmin, col = "red", lwd = 2)

qqnorm(log_T.tot, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(log_T.tot, col = "purple", lwd = 2)

qqnorm(log_size.mm, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(log_size.mm, col = "yellow", lwd = 2)

# sqrt qqnorm
qqnorm(sqrt_CTmax, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(sqrt_CTmax, col = "blue", lwd = 2)

qqnorm(sqrt_CTmin, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(sqrt_CTmin, col = "red", lwd = 2)

qqnorm(sqrt_T.tot, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(sqrt_T.tot, col = "purple", lwd = 2)

qqnorm(sqrt_size.mm, pch = 1, frame = FALSE)
qqline(sqrt_size.mm, col = "yellow", lwd = 2)

Within species

First, let’s look within species.

We’ll be using generalized linear mixed models. Mixed effects models come in two flavors: linear mixed effects and generalized linear mixed effects. In essence, linear models are used on “normal” data while generalized models can take into account small samples or non-normal distributions.

Both types of models have two types of effects: fixed effects and random effects. Fixed effects are explanatory variables (x values) that have a fixed or finite number of levels. Random effects are also explanatory variables, but that have an infinite number of levels OR are variables that we aren’t necessarily interested in, but want to account for any influence they have on the dependent variable. To illustrate the difference, check out this video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VhMWPkTbXoY&t. In this case oat type and nitrogen concentrations are fixed effects because they are explanatory variables with either 3 or 4 levels (only 3 types of oats and only 4 concentrations of nitrogen) and blocks is a random effect because there was an infinite number of possible sites they could’ve picked to make a block.

For our experiment, take a second to think, what are our fixed effects and random effects? Type your answer below.

FIXED AND RANDOM EFFECTS:

Ok, let’s get stuck into mixed models. For mixed models, you’ll need to use the following packages: lme4 and lmerTest. You will need to install and load them up (using the library() function).

Since our dataset is small, you will use the glmer() function. You will follow it up with the summary() function.

  • glmer uses the format yvalues~xvalue*xvalue[more if needed] + (1|random effect), data =dataframe

  • remember to name your mixed model so you can use it in your summary function!

#load the lme4 and lmerTest packages


#perform a mixed model on the max temp by sex and size in sailfin (don't for the random effects!)


#repeat for mosquitofish and swordtails






#perform a mixed model on the min temp by by sex and size in sailfin (don't for the random effects!)


#repeat for mosquitofish and swordtails 







#perform a mixed model on the tot temp by by sex and size in sailfin (don't for the random effects!)

#repeat for mosquitofish and swordtails 




#perform a mixed model on the max temp by mating and size in swordtails (don't for the random effects!)
     #here we are looking at courting vs coercive males

#repeat for min and tot temp

Between species

Now, let’s take a look between species.

Here we want to see if there are differences between the species within each sex. For example, did males in a courting species differ than males in a non-courting speices? Since we are only looking at one sex at a time, you may need to create two new dataframes from the raw data of only males and only females. We’ll still be using mixed models here!

#create dataframes for just males and just females


#perform a mixed model on max temp by species and size for males

#repeat for females



#perform a mixed model on min temp by species and size for males

#repeat for females



#perform a mixed model on tot temp by species and size for males

#repeat for females

What do you see in these small chunks? Do certain temperatures give different trends within different species? What differences, if any, are there between species? How about just male or just female differences?

SMALL CHUNK ANALYSIS:

Big picture

While it is nice to explore our data in the small chunks, to really address our questions we need to take a look at the whole picture. We’ll use the glmms again for this.

library(lme4)
## Loading required package: Matrix
## 
## Attaching package: 'Matrix'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:tidyr':
## 
##     expand, pack, unpack
library(lmerTest)
## 
## Attaching package: 'lmerTest'
## The following object is masked from 'package:lme4':
## 
##     lmer
## The following object is masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     step
#perform a mixed model on the max temperature across sex, size and species 
model_all.1 = lm(CTmax.C~Sex+Size.mm+Species, data = rawdata_1)
summary(model_all.1)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = CTmax.C ~ Sex + Size.mm + Species, data = rawdata_1)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.0107 -0.2922  0.1803  0.7461  1.4717 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         37.79742    0.72118  52.410  < 2e-16 ***
## Sexmale              0.27793    0.23221   1.197  0.23517    
## Size.mm             -0.01430    0.02569  -0.557  0.57939    
## SpeciesP. latipinna  0.97996    0.36354   2.696  0.00869 ** 
## SpeciesX. nigrensis -2.31496    0.31376  -7.378 1.93e-10 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 1.031 on 74 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6762, Adjusted R-squared:  0.6587 
## F-statistic: 38.63 on 4 and 74 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
#repeat for min temp and tot temp
model_all.2 = lm(CTmin.C~Sex+Size.mm+Species, data = rawdata_1)
summary(model_all.2)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = CTmin.C ~ Sex + Size.mm + Species, data = rawdata_1)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.2214 -0.7095  0.1737  0.8181  2.7771 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)          5.58372    0.99195   5.629 3.08e-07 ***
## Sexmale              0.21760    0.31940   0.681    0.498    
## Size.mm             -0.02921    0.03533  -0.827    0.411    
## SpeciesP. latipinna  2.15876    0.50003   4.317 4.83e-05 ***
## SpeciesX. nigrensis  4.89113    0.43156  11.333  < 2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 1.417 on 74 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6622, Adjusted R-squared:  0.644 
## F-statistic: 36.27 on 4 and 74 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
model_all.3 = lm(T.tot~Sex+Size.mm+Species, data = rawdata_1)
summary(model_all.3)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = T.tot ~ Sex + Size.mm + Species, data = rawdata_1)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -5.1418 -1.1982 -0.0052  1.2042  3.9745 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         32.21370    1.43006  22.526   <2e-16 ***
## Sexmale              0.06033    0.46046   0.131    0.896    
## Size.mm              0.01491    0.05094   0.293    0.771    
## SpeciesP. latipinna -1.17880    0.72087  -1.635    0.106    
## SpeciesX. nigrensis -7.20609    0.62217 -11.582   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 2.043 on 74 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.7226, Adjusted R-squared:  0.7076 
## F-statistic: 48.18 on 4 and 74 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
#perform a mixed model on the max temperature across mating, size and species
model_all.4 = lm(CTmax.C~Mating+Size.mm+Species, data = rawdata_1)
summary(model_all.4)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = CTmax.C ~ Mating + Size.mm + Species, data = rawdata_1)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -1.71972 -0.42362  0.03732  0.50118  1.29046 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)         38.29508    1.06255  36.041  < 2e-16 ***
## Matingcourting       0.63147    0.78172   0.808    0.425    
## Size.mm             -0.01432    0.04787  -0.299    0.767    
## SpeciesP. latipinna  0.02426    0.49977   0.049    0.962    
## SpeciesX. nigrensis -2.84815    0.36452  -7.813 3.52e-09 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.7285 on 35 degrees of freedom
##   (39 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.8169, Adjusted R-squared:  0.796 
## F-statistic: 39.04 on 4 and 35 DF,  p-value: 1.911e-12
#repeat for min temp and tot temp
model_all.5 = lm(CTmin.C~Mating+Size.mm+Species, data = rawdata_1)
summary(model_all.5)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = CTmin.C ~ Mating + Size.mm + Species, data = rawdata_1)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.3789 -0.7859  0.1262  0.7297  2.7410 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)          7.95063    2.08423   3.815 0.000532 ***
## Matingcourting       1.53904    1.53336   1.004 0.322411    
## Size.mm             -0.13103    0.09389  -1.396 0.171631    
## SpeciesP. latipinna  1.83021    0.98031   1.867 0.070300 .  
## SpeciesX. nigrensis  4.78423    0.71502   6.691  9.6e-08 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 1.429 on 35 degrees of freedom
##   (39 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6795, Adjusted R-squared:  0.6429 
## F-statistic: 18.55 on 4 and 35 DF,  p-value: 2.897e-08
model_all.6 = lm(T.tot~Mating+Size.mm+Species, data = rawdata_1)
summary(model_all.6)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = T.tot ~ Mating + Size.mm + Species, data = rawdata_1)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.1753 -1.1605 -0.1284  1.1299  3.4259 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)          30.3445     2.6504  11.449 2.19e-13 ***
## Matingcourting       -0.9076     1.9499  -0.465    0.644    
## Size.mm               0.1167     0.1194   0.977    0.335    
## SpeciesP. latipinna  -1.8060     1.2466  -1.449    0.156    
## SpeciesX. nigrensis  -7.6324     0.9093  -8.394 6.70e-10 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 1.817 on 35 degrees of freedom
##   (39 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.7872, Adjusted R-squared:  0.7628 
## F-statistic: 32.36 on 4 and 35 DF,  p-value: 2.571e-11
mate.max <- lm(CTmax.C~Mating, data = rawdata_1)
summary(mate.max)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = CTmax.C ~ Mating, data = rawdata_1)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -3.0360 -1.3770  0.5915  1.3586  2.1540 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)     36.6575     0.3791  96.694   <2e-16 ***
## Matingcourting   0.9385     0.5000   1.877   0.0682 .  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 1.563 on 38 degrees of freedom
##   (39 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.08486,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.06078 
## F-statistic: 3.524 on 1 and 38 DF,  p-value: 0.06819
mate.min <- lm(CTmin.C~Mating, data = rawdata_1)
summary(mate.min)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = CTmin.C ~ Mating, data = rawdata_1)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -4.2324 -1.2796  0.2878  1.9430  3.6426 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)      7.4824     0.5861  12.767 2.55e-15 ***
## Matingcourting   0.3396     0.7729   0.439    0.663    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 2.416 on 38 degrees of freedom
##   (39 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.005055,   Adjusted R-squared:  -0.02113 
## F-statistic: 0.1931 on 1 and 38 DF,  p-value: 0.6629
mate.tot <- lm(T.tot~Mating, data = rawdata_1)
summary(mate.tot)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = T.tot ~ Mating, data = rawdata_1)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -5.3651 -3.4949  0.1647  2.8507  5.8849 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)     29.1751     0.9139  31.924   <2e-16 ***
## Matingcourting   0.5989     1.2052   0.497    0.622    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 3.768 on 38 degrees of freedom
##   (39 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.006456,   Adjusted R-squared:  -0.01969 
## F-statistic: 0.2469 on 1 and 38 DF,  p-value: 0.6221

Size

For our previous models, we are accounting for the variation of size, but it isn’t the factor of interest. It is possible that size is more of a driving factor for thermal tolerance differences than sex or courtship behaviors.

#performed a mixed model on max temp by size and sex (ID only random effect)

#repeat for min and tot temp



#performed a mixed model on max temp by size (species and ID as random effects)

#repeat for min and tot temp

Results

What did you find? Did certain temperatures differ between sexes? Did they differ between species? If there was an interaction effect, what does this mean? How influential was size?

RESULTS:

Plots

It is always best to have visuals for the analyses you perform. This allows readers to visually grasp the comparisons you are making. Whenever possible, it is also a good idea to overlay the original datapoints so that readers can see how individuals change over trials or how the mean compares to the spread of the data (often this is done with faint grey points so as to not be too crowded on the graph). For your study, it might also be interesting to connect the same indiviual points so the audience can see the ranges of each fish. Lastly, it is important to label axes, add legends (if needed), add titles, and add or remove background grids to ensure the graph is easy to read and interpret. I tend to use ggplot to do all of this, but most can also be done in base R plotting. I’ll leave it up to you to decide on which one to use and to google for instructions on how to change the aesthetics. What I’ll list below is the plots you should have for your presentation (if you’ve done the plot already in summary stats, you can just copy it here).

For some tips on ggplot: * https://r-statistics.co/Complete-Ggplot2-Tutorial-Part1-With-R-Code.html * https://www.analyticsvidhya.com/blog/2022/03/a-comprehensive-guide-on-ggplot2-in-r/ * https://www.cedricscherer.com/2019/08/05/a-ggplot2-tutorial-for-beautiful-plotting-in-r/ * http://zevross.com/blog/2014/08/04/beautiful-plotting-in-r-a-ggplot2-cheatsheet-3/

For some tips on base plotting: * https://towardsdatascience.com/base-plotting-in-r-eb365da06b22 * http://www.sthda.com/english/wiki/r-base-graphs * https://hbctraining.github.io/Intro-to-R/lessons/basic_plots_in_r.html * https://www.tenderisthebyte.com/blog/2021/05/09/pretty-plots-with-base-r-grahpics/

library(ggplot2)
## Gives count, mean, standard deviation, standard error of the mean, and confidence interval (default 95%).
##   data: a data frame.
##   measurevar: the name of a column that contains the variable to be summariezed
##   groupvars: a vector containing names of columns that contain grouping variables
##   na.rm: a boolean that indicates whether to ignore NA's
##   conf.interval: the percent range of the confidence interval (default is 95%)
summarySE <- function(data=NULL, measurevar, groupvars=NULL, na.rm=FALSE,
                      conf.interval=.95, .drop=TRUE) {
    library(plyr)

    # New version of length which can handle NA's: if na.rm==T, don't count them
    length2 <- function (x, na.rm=FALSE) {
        if (na.rm) sum(!is.na(x))
        else       length(x)
    }

    # This does the summary. For each group's data frame, return a vector with
    # N, mean, and sd
    datac <- ddply(data, groupvars, .drop=.drop,
      .fun = function(xx, col) {
        c(N    = length2(xx[[col]], na.rm=na.rm),
          mean = mean   (xx[[col]], na.rm=na.rm),
          sd   = sd     (xx[[col]], na.rm=na.rm)
        )
      },
      measurevar
    )

    # Rename the "mean" column    
    datac <- rename(datac, c("mean" = measurevar))

    datac$se <- datac$sd / sqrt(datac$N)  # Calculate standard error of the mean

    # Confidence interval multiplier for standard error
    # Calculate t-statistic for confidence interval: 
    # e.g., if conf.interval is .95, use .975 (above/below), and use df=N-1
    ciMult <- qt(conf.interval/2 + .5, datac$N-1)
    datac$ci <- datac$se * ciMult

    return(datac)
}


sums.max <- summarySE(rawdata_1, measurevar="CTmax.C", groupvars=c("Sex","Species"))
## ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## You have loaded plyr after dplyr - this is likely to cause problems.
## If you need functions from both plyr and dplyr, please load plyr first, then dplyr:
## library(plyr); library(dplyr)
## ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 
## Attaching package: 'plyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     arrange, count, desc, failwith, id, mutate, rename, summarise,
##     summarize
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     compact
## The following objects are masked from 'package:rstatix':
## 
##     desc, mutate
sums.min <- summarySE(rawdata_1, measurevar="CTmin.C", groupvars=c("Sex","Species"))
sums.tot <- summarySE(rawdata_1, measurevar="T.tot", groupvars=c("Sex","Species"))

pd <- position_dodge(0.1) # move them .05 to the left and right

ggplot(sums.max, aes(x=Sex, y=CTmax.C, colour=Species, group=interaction(Species,Sex))) + 
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin=CTmax.C-ci, ymax=CTmax.C+ci), width=.1, position=pd) +
    geom_line(position=pd) +
    geom_point(position=pd) 
## `geom_line()`: Each group consists of only one observation.
## ℹ Do you need to adjust the group aesthetic?

ggplot(sums.max, aes(x=Sex, y=CTmax.C, colour=Species, group=Species)) + 
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin=CTmax.C-se, ymax=CTmax.C+se), colour="black", width=.1, position=pd) +
    geom_line(position=pd) +
    geom_point(position=pd, size=3, shape=21, fill="white") + # 21 is filled circle
    xlab("Sex") +
    ylab("Temperature (°C)") +
    scale_colour_hue(name="Species", breaks=c("G.affinis", "P.latipinna", "X.nigrensis"), labels=c("Mosquitofish", "Sailfin", "Swordtail"), l=40) + 
    ggtitle("CTmax of males and females\nacross all species") + theme_bw() + theme(legend.justification=c(1,0), legend.position=c(1,0))               

ggplot(sums.min, aes(x=Sex, y=CTmin.C, colour=Species, group=Species)) + 
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin=CTmin.C-se, ymax=CTmin.C+se), colour="black", width=.1, position=pd) +
    geom_line(position=pd) +
    geom_point(position=pd, size=3, shape=21, fill="white") + # 21 is filled circle
    xlab("Sex") +
    ylab("Temperature (°C)") +
    scale_colour_hue(name="Species", breaks=c("G.affinis", "P.latipinna", "X.nigrensis"), labels=c("Mosquitofish", "Sailfin", "Swordtail"), l=40) + 
    ggtitle("CTmin of males and females\nacross all species") + theme_bw() + theme(legend.justification=c(1,0), legend.position=c(1,0))

ggplot(sums.tot, aes(x=Sex, y=T.tot, colour=Species, group=Species)) + 
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin=T.tot-se, ymax=T.tot+se), colour="black", width=.1, position=pd) +
    geom_line(position=pd) +
    geom_point(position=pd, size=3, shape=21, fill="white") + # 21 is filled circle
    xlab("Sex") +
    ylab("Temperature difference, CTmax-CTmin (°C)") +
    scale_colour_hue(name="Species", breaks=c("G.affinis", "P.latipinna", "X.nigrensis"), labels=c("Mosquitofish", "Sailfin", "Swordtail"), l=40) + 
    ggtitle("Thermal Tolerance Range of males and females\nacross all species") + theme_bw() + theme(legend.justification=c(1,0), legend.position=c(1,0))

library(ggpubr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'ggpubr'
## The following object is masked from 'package:plyr':
## 
##     mutate
ggplot(rawdata_1, aes(x=Size.mm, y=CTmax.C, color=Sex, shape=Sex)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, formula = y~x) + stat_cor(label.x = c(33, 33), label.y = c(40.5, 41)) + theme_classic() + labs(title="CTmax by size \n for males and females",
       x="Standard length (mm)", y = "Temperature (°C)")

ggplot(rawdata_1, aes(x=Size.mm, y=CTmin.C, color=Sex, shape=Sex)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, formula = y~x) + stat_cor(label.x = c(33, 33), label.y = c(15, 15.5)) + theme_classic() + labs(title="CTmin by size \n for males and females",
       x="Standard length (mm)", y = "Temperature (°C)")

ggplot(rawdata_1, aes(x=Size.mm, y=T.tot, color=Sex, shape=Sex)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, formula = y~x) + stat_cor(label.x = c(33, 33), label.y = c(38.5, 39)) + theme_classic() + labs(title="Thermal tolerance range by size \n for males and females",
       x="Standard length (mm)", y = "Temperature difference, CTmax-CTmin (°C)")

ggplot(subset(rawdata_1, !is.na(Mating)), aes(Mating, CTmax.C, fill=Mating)) + geom_boxplot(color="black") + labs(title="CTmax for different reproductive methods", x ="Reproductive method", y= "Temperature (C)") + theme_classic() + scale_fill_manual(values = c("skyblue2", "royalblue4"))

ggplot(subset(rawdata_1, !is.na(Mating)), aes(Mating, CTmin.C, fill=Mating)) + geom_boxplot(color="black") + labs(title="CTmin for different reproductive methods", x ="Reproductive method", y= "Temperature (C)") + theme_classic() + scale_fill_manual(values = c("skyblue2", "royalblue4"))

ggplot(subset(rawdata_1, !is.na(Mating)), aes(Mating, T.tot, fill=Mating)) + geom_boxplot(color="black") + labs(title="Thermal tolernace range \n for different reproductive methods", x ="Reproductive method", y= "Temperature (C)") + theme_classic() + scale_fill_manual(values = c("skyblue2", "royalblue4"))

#male v female max temp in sailfin
sailfin_boxplot1 <- ggplot(data = sailfin_1, 
                           aes(x = Sex,
                               y = CTmax.C,
                               fill = Sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Temperatures by Sex in Sailfins",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Temperature (°C)"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue"))
  

print(sailfin_boxplot1)

#male v female max temp in mosquitofish
mosquito_boxplot1 <- ggplot(data = mosquito_1, 
                           aes(x = Sex,
                               y = CTmax.C,
                               fill = Sex)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Temperatures by Sex in Mosquito Fish",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Temperature (°C)"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue"))

print(mosquito_boxplot1)

#male v female max temp in swordtail (might want to either create another graph distinguishing between small and large males or just add size category as a shape difference)
swordtail_boxplot4 <- ggplot(data = swordtail_1, 
                             aes(x = interaction(Sex, Size.cat),
                                 y = CTmax.C,
                                 fill = interaction(Sex, Size.cat))) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Temperatures by Sex and Size in Swordtails",
    x = "Sex and Size Category",
    y = "Temperature (°C)",
    fill = "Legend"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("salmon", "blue", "forestgreen"))

print(swordtail_boxplot4)

Other

Sooooo it could be that weight is actually a bigger factor than their length. Unfortunately, we only have average weights (so average male and average female weights per species). We can’t connect them to particular individuals, so we can’t use this factor the same as we’ve done above. Tbh not 100% sure what to do here… may be something you just talk about as obvious other contributors to the differences/future studies type thing.
